reasoning: Ian Nepomniachtchi is ranked exactly #10 on the March 2025 list with a rating of 2753. To remain "as high or higher", he must maintain a Top 10 spot. In March 2025, he won the Aeroflot Open with a score of 7/9. Given typical open tournament opposition averages (~2550-2650), scoring +5 (7/9) will likely result in a minor rating change (either a slight gain or a very small loss of <3 points), easily keeping him above the players ranked #11-13 (Mamedyarov, So, Aronian) who are all sitting at 2748 with no major classical events known to cause a huge +5 jump in March. Therefore, for the April rating list (published April 1), he is highly likely to remain in the Top 10. The April 6 and April 29 dates will both almost certainly resolve to this same April 1 list, so their probabilities are very high (~88-89%). However, for later dates, Nepomniachtchi (age 34) is on the precipice of the Top 10, facing stiff competition from a rising wave of younger players (Erigaisi, Gukesh, Abdusattorov, Praggnanandhaa, Firouzja, Niemann, etc.). Over a 3-12 month horizon, variance naturally increases and he is more likely to occasionally slip out of the Top 10 into the 11-15 range. Accordingly, my probabilities monotonically decline into the 50s and then to 42% for the March 2026 forecast.
